title: Chapter 53
text: Chapter 46 Weeks Pregnant: 41 Animals on the Mountain: 12 Trista “Trista, I’m not fucking around here anymore,” Wyatt growls angrily under his breath, pointing to the barn door. “I’m starting the truck, and you will join me in five minutes or so help me God, I will carry you off this mountain.” I laugh and rub my swollen belly that’s sticking out of my Carhartt coat that I can no longer zip. “It’ll take an act of God to lift me.” “Try me.” The muscle in his jaw pops angrily as he gazes down at me with heat in his eyes. If I didn’t know any better, I’d say he likes fighting with me. Perhaps it brings back memories of him bossing me around in the early days of this pregnancy. A lot has changed since then. I can no longer see my bajingo, for one. And I’m pretty sure I’m wearing two different shoes. Wyatt stops at Millie’s pen to give her one last pat on the head before he continues on into the snowy tundra. It’s Christmas Eve and he’s been riding my ass about getting off the mountain ever since my due date has come and gone. He made plans for us to stay at Max’s house until the baby arrives because he doesn’t want to get snowed in and have me go into labor on the mountain. But I’ve been dragging my feet because I hate the idea of leaving all the animals. We are nearly at capacity on Mount Millie, as I’ve taken to calling it. Millie greets every new furry friend that comes up here, so it seems only right this peak should be named after our hostess. She loves all her new friends just as much as I do. The feeling of contentment I get seeing every stall filled with a happy animal has me fixing to burst—and not just with child. With sublime happiness. This winter has felt like I’m living in a Hallmark movie up here. It’s no wonder it’s been so hard to leave. But the more snow that comes, the grumpier my mountain man gets, so I suppose it’s time to give in. “Promise me, you’ll look at my instructions every day.” I point to the big chalkboard mounted on the wall as I heavily exhale. Just the simple act of walking is harder these days. Fighting to catch my breath, I add, “Check off each one daily so you don’t forget anyone.” “We got it, Mama Bear,” Calder drones as he feeds a bit of hay to Trumpet, the alpaca. “We’ll text you if we have questions,” Luke confirms. “We can handle this.” I turn to Reginald’s pen last, and my stomach tightens. “And don’t forget, Reggie doesn’t like to wake up early, but you still need to feed him early. If he wakes up and his trough is empty, he will be a pill for you the whole day. I’m not exaggerating. Try getting a moody, potbellied pig out of his stall so you can muck it out... It’s not easy.” Luke laughs. “We got it, Tris.” A smile lights up inside me at the use of Luke’s nickname for me. I’m Mama Bear to Calder and Tris to Luke. Nicknames are such an odd, comforting thing. Like I’m finally one of the family. “Thank you both, again.” I inhale sharply as a shooting pain courses through my belly. “You okay?” Luke stares down at me with worry. I wave him off. “Yes, I’m fine. It’s just the damn Braxton-Hicks. They’re worse than ever today.” They both continue to gape at me, but I ignore them and get back to the task at hand. “And I know you guys are coming to Max’s tomorrow for Christmas.” A harsh breath escapes my lips as pain shoots through my groin. “But remind me again that you can get back up the mountain if the snow gest really bad.” My bajingo radiates a deep ache that causes me to hunch over and grip the edge of Reggie’s pen. “Seriously, Tris, this isn’t normal.” Luke rushes over to me and places a hand on my back. I slap the wood of Reggie’s gate. “Just tell me you can get up this mountain in a storm!” “Yes, the snowmobiles are stored in the shed at the bottom of the lane,” Calder says on a rush, gripping his neck and eyeing me cautiously from a safe distance. “We know how to get up and down if needed. Now you need to get to that truck before Wyatt—” “Oh my God,” I gasp when I feel an intense wave of pressure hit me out of nowhere that’s immediately followed by a popping sensation. “I just peed my pants!” “You what?” Calder’s face is the picture of disturbed. “Oh my God, I peed my pants,” I exclaim with irritation. “I did not read that in the baby books! Ow!” Another sharp sensation roils through me, and I hunch over, tensing through something fierce. “Tris, I don’t think you peed your pants,” Luke says, looking down at my soaked jeans. “I think your water broke.” “Oh shit!” I shout as realization dawns on me. “Wyatt is going to be so mad at youuuu,” Calder sings, a cocky glint in his eye as he looks at me all hunched over and wet. “Shut up, Calder!” Luke and I both erupt in unison. “I’m just saying...he wanted you off this mountain for this exact reason, and now we’re literally about ready to have a baby Jesus in the barn on Christmas Eve.” “We are not!” I begin to make my way out of the barn, and Luke and Calder instantly rush to my sides, grabbing both arms like I’m incapable of walking. “Stop touching me. I can walk just fine on my own!” “What happened?” Wyatt roars as he sees me walking out of the barn like a total spectacle. “Mama Bear is about to have your cub,” Calder replies with a laugh. “What?” Wyatt roars with wide, terrified eyes on me. “Relax, Wyatt...it was just my water breaking.” “Holy shit!” he exclaims, and my body is jostled when he swoops me up into his arms. “I don’t need to be carried!” I argue as he jogs...literally jogs...toward the truck. He opens the back door for me while barking commands at his brothers. “Luke, you drive. Calder...you stay with the animals.” “No way am I missing this!” Calder argues as he jumps in the passenger side. “What about the animals?” I look at Calder with pleading eyes. “We just fed them. They’ll be fine until morning.” “And you guys swear you can get back up the mountain?” “Yes!” All three brothers bark at me in unison. “Then, Luke...please drive like hell because it feels like I’m about to give birth in this truck!” Wyatt begins muttering obscenities under his breath, and I know it has everything to do with the fact that he wanted to be in Boulder when this happened, not up on Mount Millie in the middle of a snowstorm. But where would the fun be in that? “Oh God,” I cry out when a wave of contractions hits me. “This hurts so bad!” Wyatt moves sideways on the seat, pulling me in between his legs and holding me close. “Lean on me and squeeze my hand, baby.” I do so, but it does nothing to stop the intense feeling pressing down on my asshole. “It feels like I need to poop.” Calder’s head snaps around to look back at me nervously. “The books said that’s how it feels right before you need to start pushing,” Wyatt says, his voice fraught with worry. “No fucking pushing!” Calder cries, turning forward and running his hand through his hair. “Luke, switch places with me. I want to drive. You can deliver the baby.” “No one is delivering the baby in this truck!” I shriek, gripping Wyatt’s leg for dear life. “Ugh, I should have listened to you, Wyatt.” “I know.” “I am so stubborn.” “I know.” “I always have to push the limits.” “I know.” “You could disagree with me on one of these, you know!” “I... Okay...you aren’t stubborn.” “You’re such a bad liar,” I whine, and a weird humming noise erupts from my chest that I’ve never heard before. “You need to drive faster, Luke,” Wyatt begs. “Do you want to get there safely or not?” Luke snarls and it’s the most heated I’ve ever seen him. His voice is low when I hear him murmur, “Why am I always the one driving to the hospitals?” I close my eyes and try my best to stay calm as we make our way down the winding roads of rural Colorado to the Boulder Medical Center. The doctor wanted to induce me a week ago, but I said no. I don’t want drugs for the delivery, and induction requires drugs. Wyatt’s right, I am a stubborn ass. How I went from never wanting children to caring about a child so much I’m willingly choosing to suffer through natural childbirth is wild. But this little goat turd healed me in so many ways. It seems only right I do the best I can by her. Come what may. We finally pull up in front of the Boulder Medical Center, where Wyatt, Calder, and Luke all damn near carry me out of the truck to place me gently into the wheelchair a nurse is holding for us. “Which one is the dad?” she asks, and Wyatt’s hand instantly shoots up while Calder and Luke stand back awkwardly. “Those two are the uncles,” I state proudly and feel my eyes sting with emotion as they both smirk proudly. “We’ll see you guys on the other side, okay?” They nod gratefully, and the nurse wheels me away with my mountain man and two of the best brothers a girl could ask for waiting in the wings. “Baby, open your eyes.” Wyatt’s deep voice breaks through the intense mental fog that has overtaken me the past forty minutes. “She’s out!” “What?” I croak and my voice sounds weird...like I’ve been screaming, but I don’t remember making any sounds. Honestly, I blacked out, I think. Like, I remember the searing pain, and I remember staring into nothing and making up a shape for each contraction, but once they put me in the stirrups and had me start pushing, I went into autopilot. It felt similar to vomiting, where my whole body just sort of took over and there was nothing I could do but just let it do its thing. If I’m being truthful, I knew I was laboring all day long on the mountain. The cramps I was feeling were different than Braxton-Hicks, but moving made them more tolerable, and I had shit to get done, so labor had to wait. The water-breaking thing just caught me off guard. “It’s a girl,” the doctor states grandly, and I have to blink my eyes multiple times to focus on the image between my legs. They fling the bony little thing up onto my chest, and I sort of stare at her on top of me, feeling bewildered and confused. The nurses aggressively wipe the goo off her, and I frown as I look to the doctor. “Is she okay?” “Yes! She’s perfect,” the doctor says on a laugh. I nod. Good, that’s good, and then my eyes find hers again. “Is my vagina okay?” She glances down between my legs. “Yep, it’s perfect too.” “Well, I know that’s a lie,” I harrumph, and faintly hear Wyatt and the nurses chuckling at me while they help him cut the umbilical cord and wrap the baby up in a tight little bundle. Finally, I pry my hands off the bed rails. I don’t know what they were doing there still. Frozen in terror, I guess? All I know is my fingers ache, so I stretch them out before I finally let myself hold my baby. My baby. She cries loudly, and on autopilot once again, I shush her before leaning down to coo softly into her ear. “There, there, baby girl. I’ve got you. Mommy’s got you.” She instantly stops crying, her little body soothed by the sound of my voice, and this strange sense of confidence overcomes me. I feel utterly certain of what I am supposed to do with this little one. She isn’t just my baby. This is my soul mate. I already know her. I’ve known her my whole life. And this moment, this magical moment, was mine from the beginning of time. “I’m sorry for the rocky journey to get here,” I whisper into her soft, delicate skin. “But you sure as heck were worth it.” I kiss her soft cheek, and a complete sense of peace and calm descends on me. A strange noise echoes above me, and I look up to find Wyatt full-on sobbing. His face is twisted up in a weird way, and I resign myself to the fact that this man is not an attractive crier. But my God, he’s beautiful. I reach up and wipe a tear off his cheek, pulling him in close. His big brawny arms wrap around me and our little girl, the warmth causing the nurses, the doctor, and whatever ungodly thing she’s doing to my “perfect” vagina to all melt away. It’s just me, my baby, my mountain man, and a happy ending beyond all my wildest dreams. Copyright © 2024 by Amy Daws
